avoiding-filtering-mistakes.label: Avoiding filtering mistakes
avoiding-filtering-mistakes.summary: Learn how to avoid common mistakes when filtering data with WHERE in SQL. Practice identifying and correcting errors in logic, missing conditions, and improper use of AND/OR.

sketch1.title: Example: Filtering customers by city and age

sk1.title: Why filtering mistakes happen
sk1.bodyMarkdown: Filtering data in SQL with the WHERE clause is powerful, but small mistakes can lead to big problems. Forgetting a WHERE clause returns all rows. Mixing up AND/OR logic can give you too many or too few results. Always double-check your conditions and use parentheses to control logic.

sk2.title: Example: Filtering customers by city and age
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
Suppose you want to find customers from 'London' who are older than 30. The correct SQL is:

```sql
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'London' AND age > 30;
```

If you forget the AND or use OR by mistake, you might get customers from other cities or all ages.

q1.title: What happens if you forget the WHERE clause?
q1.prompt: If you run `SELECT * FROM customers;` without a WHERE clause, what will happen?
q1.hint: Think about what happens when no filter is applied.

help.concept: Without a WHERE clause, SQL returns every row from the table.
help.hint_1: No filter means nothing is excluded.
help.hint_2: The query will show you every record in the customers table.

q2.title: Choosing the correct logical operator
q2.prompt: You want to find customers who live in 'Paris' and are over 40 years old. Which logical operator should you use between the conditions?
q2.hint: You want both conditions to be true at the same time.

help.concept: The the missing term operator ensures both conditions must be true for a row to be included.
help.hint_1: If you use OR, you'll get too many results.
help.hint_2: Use the missing term to require both city the missing term age conditions.

q2.template: You should use ____ between the conditions.

q3.title: Spotting a filtering mistake
q3.prompt: Which query will return all customers from 'Berlin' who are younger than 25?
q3.hint: You need both the city and age conditions to be true.

help.concept: To filter by two conditions, use AND so both must be true.
help.hint_1: OR would include customers from other cities.
help.hint_2: Make sure both city and age are filtered together.

options.a: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Berlin' AND age < 25;
options.b: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Berlin' OR age < 25;
options.c: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Berlin' AND age > 25;

q4.title: Identifying correct use of parentheses
q4.prompt: Which of the following queries correctly use parentheses to combine AND and OR conditions?
q4.hint: Parentheses help control the order of logic.

help.concept: Parentheses group conditions so SQL knows which to evaluate together.
help.hint_1: Look for queries where parentheses clarify the logic.
help.hint_2: Queries without parentheses can be ambiguous with AND/OR.

options.a: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Rome' OR (age > 50 AND status = 'active');
options.b: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE (city = 'Rome' OR age > 50) AND status = 'active';
options.c: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Rome' OR age > 50 AND status = 'active';
options.d: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE (city = 'Rome' AND age > 50) OR status = 'active';

q5.title: Write a correct filter query
q5.prompt: Write a SQL query to select all customers from the 'customers' table who live in 'Madrid' and have a status of 'inactive'.
q5.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.
